title: Part 6
text: ‘society’. Van Gennep sure enough refused Durkheim’s societal determinism. Yet it is difficult to accept how this alone would have justified a complete rupture between Durkheim and his students and van Gennep. In his argument, Belier buys into the false premise (in itself a legacy of Durkheim) that we are left with a choice to study either the individual or the collective level. As Marcel Mauss himself would come to realize (most visibly so in his works on the notion of the person and on the techniques of the body), Durkheim’s collectivist position was itself untenable. Rather than effectively sociologizing ‘Western individualism’ (as Belier suggests), Durkheim quite simply transposed the ‘closed self’ to the level of society, replacing one ‘ideology’ with another. As Victor Turner (1988: 84) would later put it, ‘Extreme individualism only understands a part of man. Extreme collectivism only understands man as a part’. Belier’s explanation of why Durkheim, Mauss and their followers could nonchalantly dismiss van Gennep’s work is quite simply not plausible. They had too many interests in common for that to be the case. How are we then to understand this disregard of van Gennep? In the 1960 Introduction written by Solon T. Kimball to the English translation of Rites de Passage it was suggested that the general failure to include van Gennep’s contribution had to do with the fact that later authors on religion (among them Durkheim) were interested in ‘quite other subjects’ than the ones treated in van Gennep’s work (Kimball, 1960: xi). This, however, is quite plainly wrong. Van Gennep’s book not only contains a masterly comparative analysis of existing ethnographic data that could not be bypassed; his analytical framework alone would have been be relevant to anyone studying religion and ritual – and this is still so today. So why, more generally, was van Gennep kept away from French academic circles? The beginning of an answer, as Zumwalt (1982) saw so clearly, is to be found in van Gennep’s opposition to Durkheim. It is with the substance of that critique that we must now engage. Van Gennep’s Critique of Durkheim: Where, When and What? Van Gennep started to develop his critique of Durkheim from early on. His first most explicitly worded critical discussion can be found in his 1906 book Mythes et Légendes, and it actually goes straight to the heart of Durkheim’s position in a surprisingly frank and direct way. And this was the problem with van Gennep’s attitude: what he thought he wrote. He was never circumspect in his critique of Durkheim. The discussion of Durkheim is part of the very long Introduction to this his second book. Before 1906, Durkheim and Mauss had written several essays on religion referring to the Australian material. So had van Gennep. It is noteworthy that whereas van Gennep refers to Mauss and Durkheim throughout (and from the very first paragraph of the Preface), as he engages his critique, he targets Durkheim Liminality and the Modern 50 only. Maybe van Gennep simply did not want to offend his former mentor and friend; but it might also indicate that he knew quite well that the problems he wished to confront belonged primarily to Durkheim and much less so to Mauss. Van Gennep’s book starts by singling out the most important works published in English, French and German on the Australian material, in a sort of user-friendly annotated bibliography. 3 It then starts to discuss the merits and shortcomings of existing approaches, and on page XXIV it is Durkheim’s turn. The critique starts off from a seemingly trivial issue, but one which was then the object of endless debate: whether it was possible to find out which descent system was the ‘original one’. Among the Arunta (one of the Australian clan societies that was also to take centre stage also in EFRL), males run most of the institutions, but at the same time they display clear signs of matrilineal descent. Durkheim had taken this as an indication and indirect proof that matrilineal descent was the original mode of social organization, and that over time it had been replaced by patrilineal descent. Van Gennep argues back that in many Australian societies parallel systems can and do co-exist in time and space, and that it is really rather risky to speculate about origins in the absence of further empirical evidence. At a more general level, van Gennep exposes Durkheim’s underlying evolutionist stance that lurks behind his allegedly sociological and ‘timeless’ explanatory apparatus. This has been pointed out again and again since, but it has rarely, if ever, been fully recognized that van Gennep was the first to raise this critique explicitly, and in direct connection to Durkheim’s empirical writings on religion. Durkheim wanted to cut off historical explanations from sociological science, but the moment he sets out to theorize himself, he plunges into evolutionist speculation. Van Gennep therefore also questions the adopted analytical procedure by which Durkheim (and in this he was not alone, of course) posits the Arunta at a certain level or stage of ‘development’, allowing him an analytical short-cut to the question of ‘origins’. Whenever Durkheim recognizes a change, over time, or between one group and another (in kinship affiliations, for example), he systematically prevents any real account of such a transformation, relegating it simply to the ‘general needs of society’ (ibid.: XXV). As van Gennep points out again and again, Durkheim leaves it utterly unexplained what those ‘needs’ really amount to and how they emerge. There is no grounding epistemology to tell us what such a ‘society’ is to be able to ‘have’ such needs. In fact, says van Gennep, what Durkheim operates is a peculiar kind of ‘métaphysique sociologique’ (1906: XXIV). Durkheim posits a metaphysical abstraction at the core of his argument, and then he artificially ‘animates’ it (ibid.: XXV), granting ‘society’ explanatory powers without ever accounting for its existence. As van Gennep says, rather provocatively, this means to resolve a problem without having even managed to pose it as a problem (ibid.). 3 Even Andrew Lang, in his otherwise critical review published in Man (van Gennep had criticized Lang’s approach quite extensively), had to admit that as a corpus on Australian myths and legends, the book held great value (Lang 1906: 126). Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 51 Very fundamentally, van Gennep puts his finger on Durkheim’s reductionist stance which simplified everything as a need and function of the primordial social entity called ‘society’. But here he raises the stakes, evidencing the connections to the not-so-unrelated political ideologisms that so easily follow from almost any strongly argued collectivist theory: for what is lurking behind this sociologism is something even deeper and more problematic, and goes way beyond the classification of Australian kinship. Van Gennep returns to Durkheim and sums up his critique on p. XXXV, in a subsection on ‘Social Modifications’, in a paragraph that is worth quoting in full: We have seen how M. Durkheim explains social modifications by the ‘needs of society’ without indicating neither the why nor the where of those needs, and without justifying how exactly a ‘society’, however small, may have ‘needs’ in the first place. It is by an identical process of animation that they speak to us of ‘the call of the fatherland’, or ‘the voice of the race’. M. Durkheim anthropomorphizes, even if this is what he pretends to defend himself from. (emphasis and inverted commas in the original, my translation) 4 According to van Gennep, Durkheim’s sociology was not just flawed at the theoretical level; the entire epistemology onto which it built bore resemblances to other and much more serious political essentialisms. Here it must be stressed very explicitly that van Gennep was of course not implying that Durkheim was ‘racist’ or ‘nationalist’ in any direct or simplistic meaning of those terms. Durkheim had strongly condemned racism and anti-Semitism, and certainly not only because of his own Jewish background. 5 Something else was at stake, and it concerned the appreciation of concrete living human beings, acting within the limits of their social and physical environments. Van Gennep argued that Durkheim’s categorical collectivism lost sight of real human beings. When Gabriel Tarde was still alive, he called this Durkheim’s tendency to dangerously construct society as a ‘divine Being’, sacrificing the individual at its altar. Durkheim now found himself with a new opponent, re-launching Tarde’s critique from another angle. And this was not just any opponent: it was someone who knew the ethnographic material onto which Durkheim had projected to construct his universalist theories of religion far better than himself. But he was also much younger and held no position. Durkheim answered van Gennep with silence; but there can be very little 4 This passage is evidently mistranslated in Zumwalt’s article from 1982, and is therefore quite important to get right, as it is possibly the earliest critique of Durkheim’s work on religion that exists. 5 The question of nationalism is more complex. As we know, Durkheim became an ardent and patriotic defender of the French Republic during the Great War. As Dingley (2008: 42) argued, in Durkheim’s works, ‘the individual was a social reality, the product of a society that was itself a reality, and for Durkheim the implicit reality of society was the nation, particularly the French nation’. Liminality and the Modern 52 doubt that he discussed the critique with Mauss. It is likely that he left it for Mauss to deal with. Arnold van Gennep’s 1913 Review of The Elementary Forms of Religious Life Van Gennep did not waste his time just discussing Durkheim; most of what he published after 1906 consisted of constructive attempts to make sense of a variety of ethnographic data, including ritual forms and passages as discussed in Rites of Passage. His many book reviews written in that period would always try to distil whatever could be said about somebody else’s work that was positive. But when Durkheim published EFRL van Gennep simply could not keep quiet. And by then he also had little reason to do so: he was by now working in Switzerland, and at a safe distance from French academic power struggles. Or, so he thought. EFRL is in fact the one book that more than any other should have discussed Rites of Passage, and for several reasons. Durkheim’s most important anthropological work was written during the period when van Gennep’s work had been made public. Mauss, who did much (some say most) of the work for EFRL, had closely followed van Gennep’s work during the year-long preparation of the material. Just like van Gennep, Durkheim famously singled out ritual as central to the constitution of not only religion but also society itself. However, Durkheim made no use of the terms or distinctions suggested in Rites de Passage. Van Gennep is dismissively (a ‘drawback’) mentioned in a footnote to Book II, Chapter 2, with a reference to an article he published in 1908 on totemism and the comparative method in Revue de l’Histoire des Religions (Durkheim 1995: 136). Van Gennep’s book Tabou et Totemisme is mentioned (but not discussed) once, in a footnote to Book III, Chapter 1 (ibid.: 324) as one of several works that document the contagiousness of the sacred. Durkheim does not consider how van Gennep further developed this quite crucial point about contagion in his larger classification of rites undertaken in Rites of Passage. When Durkheim in Book II goes into some detail on the myths and legends that underpin Australian totemic beliefs, Mythes et légendes d’Australie. Etude d’ethnographie et de sociologie by van Gennep – a whole book dedicated to the ethnographic case study that grounds the entire argument of EFRL – is not once referred to. In this founding work of sociology, Durkheim does not find it worthwhile even to discuss van Gennep, possibly the person in France who knew most about the substance of EFRL. Why not? Two possible reasons immediately present themselves: Either Durkheim had not read van Gennep’s work, or he had read it but found it of no interest or value. Both reasons can relatively safely be discarded; and we know with certainty that Mauss had read them. Moreover, EFRL carried the subtitle Le système totémique en Australie, and van Gennep had in 1904 published his first book on the very topic of totemism, Tabou et Totémisme à Madagascar. This was a book that Marcel Mauss had proofread and contributed to, and that contained an important (if early and still developing) theoretical discussion of totemism. Durkheim knew van Gennep’s work beyond any reasonable doubt, and it is likely that Mauss felt a certain degree Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 53 of unease omitting a more thorough discussion of van Gennep. They had studied many of the texts on totemism and primitive religion together. Van Gennep published his review of EFRL in Mercure de France, on January 16, 1913 (van Gennep 2001: 92–5). EFRL is still today considered the classic work within the sociology of religion, and whether we like it or not, it is where many of our discussions start. However much we choose to deviate from EFRL, it has in many ways taken hold of our imaginary boundaries of the subject field. The question we seriously have to ask is whether this book should not be considered a genuinely false start. At the very least, when we today make reference to Durkheim’s work on religion (and use it as the standard text in our university courses) we jointly ought to include and consider van Gennep’s early critique. Van Gennep’s review of EFRL neatly sums up, in three pages, everything that he finds problematic with Durkheim’s exposé. As is well-known, EFRL is composed of two parts; one is general-theoretical, the other is monographic. The latter, starts van Gennep, is the weakest part. Van Gennep feels more than entitled to engage with this ethnographic part, for as he says, he knows each and every one of Durkheim’s sources, having used them himself in previous works. Van Gennep states quite bluntly that Durkheim demonstrates a complete lack of any critical stance towards his sources, collected by traders, police agents and priests, naively accepting their veracity. Durkheim over-states the theoretical potential of single facts and interprets freely from dubious data. It is this ‘fictional’ procedure that Durkheim falsely attributes to the ‘German’ method. 6 In every single line of the book, says van Gennep, one feels tempted to dot down a simple but legitimate question mark: ‘Is he right about what he is saying?’, ‘What are his sources?’, ‘How can we actually know that what he argues corresponds to reality?’. Van Gennep was himself an extremely meticulous note-taker and bibliographer, and as a reviewer he would confront any academic work with the same criticism: the moment it failed to be transparent about sources, their reliability and veracity, and the logical steps made in one’s argumentation, he would point it out, warning the reader that the conclusions reached could not be verified and therefore may not stand up to scrutiny. Durkheim’s book did not pass the test on this first, fundamental measure of accuracy and reliability. In other words, van Gennep was quite fundamentally challenging Durkheim on his empirical data, technical knowledge and scientific transparency. He therefore states the first part of the conclusions categorically and definitively: Within ten years, his entire systematisation of the Australian material will be completely rejected, and with them the generalizations that he has constructed on the basis of the most fragile ethnographic data I have ever seen. The view he has proposed concerning ‘primitive man’ and ‘simple’ societies is entirely erroneous. (ibid.: 92, my translation) 6 Durkheim had had a crucial stay in Germany in 1885–1886, and liked to see himself as the transmitter of German empiricism and neo-Kantianism to the French universities. Liminality and the Modern 54 This was no minor issue, since Durkheim’s project and his very academic identity rested upon a reputation of being scientific and empirically founded. What Durkheim knew of ethnography he mostly had from Mauss. His command of the material was very partial. Van Gennep, better than anyone else, was in a position to spot Durkheim’s lack of expertise in ethnography, and Durkheim’s tendency to press the ethnography into a prefabricated theoretical scheme. The plain fact is that Durkheim’s insistence upon using ‘facts’ and ‘observable social phenomena’ for theory building was simply not followed up in his own work; Arnold van Gennep pointed this out more clearly than anyone else has ever done since. And he did so without the privilege of hindsight. For that reason alone, sociologists should start to take van Gennep much more seriously. Here of course, one may legitimately come to the rescue of Durkheim: does his theoretical attempt not deserve to be taken seriously, irrespective of a superficially analysed and flawed empirical material? Van Gennep would agree: Durkheim’s general theories on totemism and on religion deserve to be considered in their own right, and are in fact full of ‘solid truths’ (ibid.: 93). Chapter V, a critique of existing theories on totemism is ‘simple, rapid, exact’ (we can be quite sure that Mauss composed most of this chapter). And yet, when Durkheim in the next chapter (on the origins of totemic beliefs) moves on to suggest his own theory, no real theory is ever proposed. What Durkheim offers, says van Gennep, is a definition of totemism (Durkheim famously proposes to see totemism as representing an anonymous and impersonal ‘force’). More than explaining anything, this definition only raises a series of question marks concerning the nature of totemism. It also misrecognizes what van Gennep had argued to be the essentially ‘energetic’ nature of religious conceptions (this is in fact yet another of van Gennep’s ideas that Mauss would later take over). Durkheim, said van Gennep, claimed to have established the ‘foundations of society’ from a single religious institution (totemism), without realizing that this was just one very specific type of classification, peculiar to this not-so-simple society. The aborigines simply cannot be posited as a ‘first’ or ‘elementary’ building block, onto which one can erect an entire edifice. These are the fantasies of a desk-scholar. As van Gennep would stress in other contexts, the aborigines (here, in particular, the Arunta) actually have rather complex matrimonial rules and rather complex totemic beliefs and practices. Even if one wants to defend an evolutionist perspective, these people simply cannot be taken to represent some kind of ursprung. It is the very foundation of Durkheim’s entire argument that is thoroughly unsound. Van Gennep also denudes Durkheim in another way, returning to one of the points raised in his 1906 discussion. Durkheim had programmatically stated his wish to liberate social explanations from historical ones. And yet, when Durkheim lists the various constitutive parts of a religious system (such as the variety of rites), instead of actually understanding them or their ‘function’, he uses them to construct, again and again, a speculative theory about their ‘origins’ (ibid.: 94). Furthermore, and just as seriously, in his insistence on staking all on the collective level, Durkheim completely and categorically neglects the action of Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 55 single, concrete individuals in the formation of institutions and beliefs. In doing so, the very process and production that lies behind myth-telling and ritual acting is annulled and hidden away. 7 It was this process formation that van Gennep had in fact tried to discuss in his own 1906 book, Mythes et Légendes – a book, says van Gennep, that Durkheim has ‘conscienscieusement neglected’ (my italics; one notes here van Gennep’s deep bitterness but also utter surprise; and he is, of course, targeting Mauss just as well, for we know from the letters from Durkheim to Mauss that it was the latter who did most of the literature reviewing). Even in the most ‘primitive’ societies, van Gennep insists, individuals do act. Durkheim dreams of assigning society a natural reality with its own laws of necessity, in a world deprived of concrete human beings. For the purposes of his theoretical construct, van Gennep continues, Durkheim has artificially reduced Australian society to a ‘monocellular organism’, devoid of agency. Durkheim possesses nothing but a metaphysic and scholastic understanding of the world; he constructs reality from pre-conceived words and concepts (ibid.). And then follows what is perhaps the most emblematic statement ever made about Durkheim and his entire sociology: Not having the sense of life, that is to say the biological and ethnographic sense, he turns phenomena and living beings into scientifically dissected plants, as in an herbarium. (ibid.: 94) It is not simply the data that is wrong; it is not the flawed methodology; it is not even the circular, redundant nature of Durkheim’s theory that is the issue: the problem is that Durkheim lacks a sense of life. This statement boils down the fundamental problem that van Gennep had identified before anyone else: Durkheim’s project did not even belong to the life sciences. It was lifeless and futile at the best; theoretically misleading and politically dangerous at the worst. 7 In fact, the telling of myths is probably one of those activities that actually require individual originality and impetus. Myths, like songs or poems and most art-forms, simply cannot be produced by collectivities. They are reproduced by collectivities – but that is a different matter. Here we must return to Weber’s premise, stated in the Introduction to The Social Psychology of the World Religions. Here Weber starts from the empirical fact that human beings are differently qualified in a religious way, and says that this fact ‘stands at the beginning of the history of religion’ (Weber 1948: 287). Weber claims that certain ecstatic and visionary capacities, a ‘charisma’, might be awakened in some but not in all (it is in this context that Weber talks about the masses – and he clearly seems to include himself – being religiously ‘unmusical’). In other words, Weber takes status stratification for granted at the basis of both political and religious movements: there is ‘heroic’ or ‘virtuoso’ religiosity as opposed to mass religiosity. Van Gennep always had a keen eye for these given differentials; in one of his lesser known essays, he discusses why some people just happen to have a good sense of direction, counting himself among the lucky ones. Van Gennep was himself a genius with languages, but he was not good at mathematics, no matter how hard he tried. Liminality and the Modern 56 It was this same recognition that 50 years later would compel Victor Turner to look for alternative frameworks which would allow him to start over again, addressing social life and its drama. The conclusion is that ‘M. Durkheim’, in his eagerness to explain religion, simply has replaced it with his own prefabricated ‘system’, his inherently impossible sociological imperative. And it is this entire enterprise that van Gennep declares useless and void. Radcliffe-Brown’s Critique There is no doubt that van Gennep’s discussion of Durkheim is the sharpest and most to the point critique ever formulated. He continued his discussion of Durkheim in a series of articles on totemism, and some of these laid the framework for the book on totemism, L’état actuel du problème totémique, van Gennep’s main thesis for his doctorate of letters, published in 1920. 8 After that, and coinciding with his abandonment of sociology and anthropology, van Gennep mostly gave up on the fight. It was a lost cause; all he could do was to follow through with his own principles within folklore. 9 While van Gennep’s review stands out for its sharpness and frank use of speech, it is worth remembering that he was not completely alone in this critique. In August 1912 Radcliffe-Brown (who also knew the ethnographic material rather well) wrote a letter to Mauss in which he expressed his disappointment with Durkheim’s superficial and erroneous treatment of the ethnographic data. Radcliffe-Brown, although (unsurprisingly) praising the general approach taken by the Durkheimian school, wrote quite plainly ‘that he [Durkheim] has misunderstood the real nature of Australian social organization’. Furthermore, Durkheim has also ‘exaggerated the importance of the clan-emblem’ (Radcliffe- Brown in Lukes 1985: 527). It should be remembered that Durkheim’s book essentially argues how social organization is the foundation of religious classification and cosmology; Durkheim’s central notion of religion as ‘self-worship’, heralded by sociologists for a century, furthermore derives very directly from his analysis of the clan-emblem. 8 In this book van Gennep reiterates why and how totemism, as a product of human ingenuity, is a both complex and extremely varied phenomenon. Therefore, he says (and with evident reference to Durkheim), ‘to explain it with verbal formulas such as collective thought, totemic mode of thought, socialization of affective values, is to retrogress to the days of phlogiston, if not to those of virtus dormitiva’ (as in Belmont 1979: 30). 9 Van Gennep did occasionally return to the shortcomings of Durkheim, and one of his more humoristic comments is found in a 1934 article on the methodology of folklore: ‘When one thinks that Durkheim and others based universal theories on tribes comprising no more than twenty to a hundred individuals, one is assailed by qualms. In Savoy I have been dealing with three million people. At that rate I could have invented a hundred universal theories just by concentrating on the exceptions’ (as translated and quoted in Belmont 1979: 56–7). Van Gennep was still biting, but no sociologist would have been reading him by then. And this he knew. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 57 According to Radcliffe-Brown he was wrong. In his friendly letter to Mauss, Radcliffe-Brown expressed a wish that Durkheim’s general ideas might start to spread in England, as they were either unknown or misperceived, but he then adds: ‘It is to be hoped that the new book will do something to alter this, but I am sorry, for that very reason, that it should contain much that I cannot help but regard as misinterpretation of the real facts’ (ibid.: 528). According to Radcliffe-Brown, Durkheim simply had the facts wrong. This authoritative statement came, not from an opponent, but from Durkheim’s closest follower in Anglo-Saxon anthropology and indeed the person who more than anyone else would establish functionalism as a dominant paradigm in anthropology. In fact, this was a critique that Durkheim could not drown in silence. Durkheim wrote two letters to Radcliffe-Brown thanking him for sharing his views on science; Durkheim added that Radcliffe-Brown’s critique made him realize that he should have a second look at the ethnographic data and reconsider his interpretation of it. That never happened (see Lukes 1985: 528–9), arguably also because of the war. Despite the fact that part of van Gennep’s critique was identical to that of Radcliffe-Brown (in his review, van Gennep suggested very directly that the book could gain much in value if some of the worst errors were corrected and the data updated), Durkheim never once bothered to respond in that direction. Mauss had contributed significantly to Durkheim’s book, and yet he mostly shied away from the debates that followed its publication. Mauss would later reveal that he himself had been unconvinced about some of Durkheim’s positions (Fournier 2006: 162). Unfortunately, Mauss would remain very indirect and reserved about those matters as long as he lived. Rites of Passage as Compared to The Elementary Forms of Religious Life As can be sensed by now, van Gennep’s outcast position in French academia probably did have something to do with his rebellious personality. It most certainly had everything to do with his theoretical and methodological approach, which was indeed quite different from that of Durkheim. Van Gennep’s work can to a large extent be understood in contrast to the ambitions and intentions of Durkheim and the Année Sociologique group. This has some importance for understanding his terminology, and the crucial role that the concept of liminality had for him, even as he turned to folklore. Four concrete differential factors can be spelled out, positing here Durkheim’s EFRL in contrast to van Gennep’s Rites of Passage. In EFRL Durkheim started out with a series of analytical distinctions, arranging religious phenomena into two fundamental categories: beliefs and rites (Durkheim 1995: 34). Rather than looking at the forms or passages present in rites, Durkheim claimed that ‘rites can be defined and distinguished [...] only by the special nature of their object’ (ibid.). That ‘object’, says Durkheim outright, is defined via the beliefs that the rite expresses. And, of course, that belief, the ‘totem’, is to Durkheim nothing other than society itself. This suggestion Liminality and the Modern 58 pre-empted van Gennep’s more fine-tuned attention to forms and patterns in rites (van Gennep used the French term schema for what was translated as ‘pattern’ in the English edition). In terms of procedure the two books set off from two very diverse starting points. Durkheim established a priori categories as the units of his taxonomy, while van Gennep inferred these units from the structure of the ceremonies themselves. Concerning the larger role of rites in society, Durkheim conceptually limited the transformative effects of rites, as he stressed the way in which rituals served to tie together individuals in mechanical social solidarity. For Durkheim, rites were simply the vectors by which individuals became socially determined as acting and thinking beings. Durkheim distinguished between religion as collective and magic as private. Durkheim here missed another crucial point about rites that van Gennep stressed throughout Rites of Passage, namely the way in which they may act simultaneously at the individual and collective levels. Moreover, while neophytes undergo a process of undifferentiation as they are ‘annulled’ as persons in the separation rituals, ritual passages are clearly also crucial moments for a process of differentiation, of age groups, of genders, of status groups, and of personalities. The use of specialized languages in ritual was to van Gennep a clear sign of this ‘differentiating procedure’ (1960: 169). In Durkheim’s analysis, individuals simply dissipate into the social body. This relates to another fundamental difference,
